1:
"Peter, an apostle of Jesus Christ, to the strangers scattered throughout Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia," The witnesses note the apostolic address to the scattered believers. Gill notes the writer describes himself by his name, Peter, the same with Cephas, which signifies a rock or stone, a name given him by Christ at his first conversion, respecting his after firmness, solidity, and constancy. The witnesses note Peter writing as an apostle of Jesus Christ to the strangers scattered through Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia; the salutation to the dispersed pilgrims of the faith — that Peter, naming himself "an apostle of Jesus Christ" (one sent by Christ's own authority), addresses his letter to "the strangers scattered" — the believers, chiefly converted Jews, dispersed through the provinces of Asia Minor (Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia) — who were "strangers" both as foreigners scattered from their homeland and, more deeply, as pilgrims and sojourners in this world, their true home being heaven; so that the letter is addressed to a suffering, dispersed people, to comfort and establish them in their pilgrim faith; to the strangers scattered throughout Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia. The witnesses note Peter, an apostle of Jesus Christ. The witnesses note to the strangers scattered throughout Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia.

The witnesses note the apostolic address to scattered believers — pilgrims and sojourners dispersed through Asia Minor, strangers both from their homeland and in this world, their home being heaven.

So the witnesses make the verse the salutation to dispersed pilgrims. The lesson the expositors draw is that Peter writes as Christ's apostle to suffering, scattered believers, to establish them in their pilgrim faith. The witnesses note the apostle: "Peter, an apostle of Jesus Christ" — Gill notes Peter, a rock, sent by Christ's authority; the apostle; the Peter; the a rock; the Christ's authority; the sent. The expositors observe the name: the name Peter signifies firmness and constancy; the name; the Peter; the firmness; the constancy; the the rock. The witnesses press the strangers: "to the strangers scattered" — the dispersed believers, chiefly converted Jews; the strangers; the scattered; the dispersed; the converted Jews; the pilgrims. The expositors draw the lesson on the the provinces: through Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, Bithynia; the the provinces; the Asia Minor; the dispersed; the scattered; the the regions. The witnesses observe the pilgrims in the world: strangers and sojourners in this world, their home in heaven; the pilgrims in the world; the sojourners; the their home; the heaven; the strangers. The expositors note the to comfort: to comfort and establish a suffering, scattered people; the to comfort; the establish; the suffering; the scattered; the the people. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Peter an apostle of Jesus Christ, the to the strangers scattered throughout Pontus Galatia Cappadocia Asia and Bithynia, and the salutation. The verse thus records the salutation to dispersed pilgrims — teaching that Peter writes as Christ's apostle to suffering, scattered believers, to establish them in their pilgrim faith.
2:
"Elect according to the foreknowledge of God the Father, through sanctification of the Spirit, unto obedience and sprinkling of the blood of Jesus Christ: Grace unto you, and peace, be multiplied." The witnesses note the trinitarian work in the election of the saints. Gill notes the persons addressed are "elect" — not to any office, nor as a nation, nor as a church, but personally and individually chosen, according to the foreknowledge of God the Father. The witnesses note Peter describing them as elect according to the foreknowledge of the Father, through the sanctification of the Spirit, unto obedience and the sprinkling of Christ's blood; the threefold divine work that brings the elect to salvation — that Peter sets the whole Trinity behind the believers' salvation: they are "elect according to the foreknowledge of God the Father" (chosen in God's eternal, loving foreknowledge), "through sanctification of the Spirit" (set apart and made holy by the Holy Spirit's work), "unto obedience and sprinkling of the blood of Jesus Christ" (the end being a life of obedience and the cleansing application of Christ's atoning blood); the witnesses note the rich order — the Father chooses, the Spirit sanctifies, the Son's blood is sprinkled, each Person concurring in the believer's salvation; and Peter prays grace and peace be multiplied to them; elect according to the foreknowledge of God the Father. The witnesses note elect according to the foreknowledge of God the Father, through sanctification of the Spirit. The witnesses note unto obedience and sprinkling of the blood of Jesus Christ.

The witnesses note the trinitarian work of salvation — elect by the Father's foreknowledge, sanctified by the Spirit, unto obedience and the sprinkling of Christ's blood; each Person concurring.

So the witnesses make the verse the threefold divine work behind election. The lesson the expositors draw is that the Father chooses, the Spirit sanctifies, and the Son's blood is sprinkled, each Person concurring in the believer's salvation. The witnesses note the elect: "elect according to the foreknowledge of God the Father" — Gill notes personally chosen in God's foreknowledge; the elect; the chosen; the the foreknowledge; the the Father; the eternal. The expositors observe the loving foreknowledge: chosen in God's eternal, loving foreknowledge; the loving foreknowledge; the eternal; the the Father; the chosen; the love. The witnesses press the sanctification: "through sanctification of the Spirit" — set apart and made holy by the Spirit; the sanctification; the the Spirit; the set apart; the made holy; the the work. The expositors draw the lesson on the obedience: "unto obedience" — the end being a life of obedience; the obedience; the the end; the a holy life; the the fruit; the obey. The witnesses observe the sprinkling of blood: "and sprinkling of the blood of Jesus Christ" — the cleansing application of Christ's atoning blood; the sprinkling of blood; the the blood; the atoning; the cleansing; the applied. The expositors note the each Person: the Father, Spirit, and Son each concur in salvation; the each Person; the the Father; the the Spirit; the the Son; the concur. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the elect according to the foreknowledge of God the Father through sanctification of the Spirit, the unto obedience and sprinkling of the blood of Jesus Christ, and the trinitarian work. The verse thus records the threefold divine work behind election — teaching that the Father chooses, the Spirit sanctifies, and the Son's blood is sprinkled, each concurring in salvation.
3:
"Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, which according to his abundant mercy hath begotten us again unto a lively hope by the resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead," The witnesses note the blessing of God for the new birth unto a living hope. Gill notes the epistle begins with thanksgiving to God, an ascription of blessing, praise, and glory to him; this does not mean invoking or conferring a blessing on God, for there is none greater to do so. The witnesses note Peter blessing the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who according to his abundant mercy has begotten us again to a living hope by Christ's resurrection; the praise to God for the new birth unto a living hope grounded in the resurrection — that Peter opens with adoration: "blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ" — and the ground of the praise is the great mercy of regeneration: God "according to his abundant mercy" (the rich, overflowing mercy that is the only spring of it) "hath begotten us again" (the new birth, making us his children) "unto a lively hope" (a living, vigorous hope of glory) "by the resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead"; the witnesses note that the believer's hope is "lively" — alive and sure — precisely because it rests on the risen Christ, whose resurrection is the pledge and ground of the believer's own; blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ. The witnesses note according to his abundant mercy hath begotten us again unto a lively hope. The witnesses note by the resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead.

The witnesses note the praise to God for the new birth — begotten again to a living hope by Christ's resurrection; the hope alive and sure because it rests on the risen Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the new birth unto a living hope. The lesson the expositors draw is that God, of his abundant mercy, has begotten believers again to a living hope grounded in Christ's resurrection. The witnesses note the blessed: "blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ" — Gill notes an ascription of praise to God; the blessed; the praise; the the God and Father; the of Christ; the adoration. The expositors observe the abundant mercy: "according to his abundant mercy" — the rich, overflowing mercy, the only spring; the abundant mercy; the rich; the overflowing; the the spring; the the source. The witnesses press the begotten again: "hath begotten us again" — the new birth, making us his children; the begotten again; the the new birth; the regeneration; the his children; the made. The expositors draw the lesson on the lively hope: "unto a lively hope" — a living, vigorous hope of glory; the lively hope; the living; the vigorous; the of glory; the sure. The witnesses observe the by the resurrection: "by the resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead" — grounded in the risen Christ; the by the resurrection; the the risen Christ; the the pledge; the the ground; the rests. The expositors note the alive and sure: the hope is alive and sure because Christ is risen; the alive and sure; the the risen Christ; the the pledge; the his own; the resurrection. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the according to his abundant mercy hath begotten us again unto a lively hope, the by the resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead, and the new birth. The verse thus records the new birth unto a living hope — teaching that God, of his abundant mercy, has begotten believers again to a living hope grounded in Christ's resurrection.
4:
"To an inheritance incorruptible, and undefiled, and that fadeth not away, reserved in heaven for you," The witnesses note the heavenly inheritance to which the saints are born. Gill notes this is a further explanation of the lively hope; it is an "inheritance," a large estate and rich possession, to which the regenerate are born heirs apparent — not got by industry, but obtained by grace. The witnesses note Peter describing the inheritance as incorruptible, undefiled, unfading, and reserved in heaven; the imperishable, kept heavenly inheritance of the believer — that Peter sets out the glorious object of the living hope: an "inheritance" (a possession the believer holds not by his own works but as a born heir of God) — and he marks its surpassing qualities by three negatives that set it above all earthly estates: "incorruptible" (not subject to decay or destruction, as earthly things are), "and undefiled" (pure, unstained, untouched by any defilement), "and that fadeth not away" (never withering or losing its beauty, unlike the fairest earthly flower) — and its security: "reserved in heaven for you" (kept safe and laid up in heaven, beyond all reach of loss); so that the believer's portion is an imperishable, pure, unfading, and securely-kept heavenly inheritance; an inheritance incorruptible, and undefiled, and that fadeth not away. The witnesses note an inheritance incorruptible, and undefiled, and that fadeth not away. The witnesses note reserved in heaven for you.

The witnesses note the heavenly inheritance — incorruptible, undefiled, unfading, reserved in heaven; an imperishable, pure, securely-kept portion above all earthly estates.

So the witnesses make the verse the imperishable heavenly inheritance. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer's portion is an incorruptible, pure, unfading inheritance kept safe in heaven. The witnesses note the inheritance: "to an inheritance" — Gill notes a possession held as a born heir of God; the inheritance; the possession; the a born heir; the of God; the not by works. The expositors observe the incorruptible: "incorruptible" — not subject to decay; the incorruptible; the no decay; the no destruction; the imperishable; the lasting. The witnesses press the undefiled: "and undefiled" — pure, unstained, untouched by defilement; the undefiled; the pure; the unstained; the no defilement; the clean. The expositors draw the lesson on the fadeth not: "and that fadeth not away" — never withering, unlike earthly flowers; the fadeth not; the never withering; the no fading; the the flower; the unfading. The witnesses observe the reserved in heaven: "reserved in heaven for you" — kept safe and laid up in heaven; the reserved in heaven; the kept safe; the laid up; the secure; the beyond loss. The expositors note the above earthly estates: this portion is above all earthly estates; the above earthly estates; the surpassing; the the negatives; the earthly; the heavenly. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the an inheritance incorruptible and undefiled and that fadeth not away, the reserved in heaven for you, and the heavenly inheritance. The verse thus records the imperishable heavenly inheritance — teaching that the believer's portion is an incorruptible, pure, unfading inheritance kept safe in heaven.
5:
"Who are kept by the power of God through faith unto salvation ready to be revealed in the last time." The witnesses note that the heirs are kept by God's power unto salvation. Gill notes this describes the persons for whom the inheritance is reserved: they are not only chosen and begotten again, but preserved unto it; their inheritance is safe in heaven, and they are kept safe on earth. The witnesses note Peter declaring that they are kept by the power of God through faith unto salvation ready to be revealed in the last time; the double security — the inheritance kept for them, and they kept for it — that Peter completes the believer's security: not only is the inheritance "reserved in heaven" (v.4), but the heirs themselves "are kept by the power of God" — guarded and preserved by God's almighty power, that they may not fall away or be lost — "through faith" (the means by which God keeps them, the grace through which his power works) "unto salvation ready to be revealed in the last time"; the witnesses note the perfect safety of the believer — the inheritance is kept for them, and they are kept for the inheritance, both secured by the power of God, until the full salvation is revealed at the last day; so that the saints' final perseverance rests on God's omnipotence, not their own strength; who are kept by the power of God through faith unto salvation. The witnesses note who are kept by the power of God through faith. The witnesses note unto salvation ready to be revealed in the last time.

The witnesses note the double security — the inheritance kept for them, and they kept by God's power through faith; the believer's final perseverance resting on God's omnipotence, not his own strength.

So the witnesses make the verse the keeping of the heirs by God's power. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believers are kept by God's almighty power through faith, that they may not be lost, until salvation is revealed. The witnesses note the kept: "who are kept by the power of God" — Gill notes preserved by God's almighty power; the kept; the preserved; the God's power; the guarded; the almighty. The expositors observe the not fall away: that they may not fall away or be lost; the not fall away; the not lost; the preserved; the secured; the guarded. The witnesses press the through faith: "through faith" — the means by which God keeps them; the through faith; the the means; the the grace; the his power works; the kept. The expositors draw the lesson on the unto salvation: "unto salvation" — kept unto the full salvation; the unto salvation; the the full; the kept; the the end; the secured. The witnesses observe the last time: "ready to be revealed in the last time" — revealed at the last day; the last time; the the last day; the revealed; the ready; the the end. The expositors note the double security: the inheritance kept for them, they kept for it; the double security; the the inheritance; the kept for them; the they kept; the both. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the who are kept by the power of God through faith, the unto salvation ready to be revealed in the last time, and the double security. The verse thus records the keeping of the heirs by God's power — teaching that the believers are kept by God's almighty power through faith, that they may not be lost, until salvation is revealed.
6:
"Wherein ye greatly rejoice, though now for a season, if need be, ye are in heaviness through manifold temptations:" The witnesses note the rejoicing of the saints amid present trials. Gill notes the saints greatly rejoice in their salvation, hope, and inheritance, though now for a season, if need be, they are in heaviness through manifold temptations. The witnesses note Peter observing that they greatly rejoice in this, though now for a season, if need be, they are in heaviness through manifold trials; the joy of salvation held even amid the grief of many trials — that Peter joins joy and sorrow in the believer's present state: "wherein ye greatly rejoice" (in the salvation, hope, and inheritance just described, the saints have a deep and abiding joy) — "though now for a season" (the trials being but for a short time) "if need be" (only so far as God sees necessary, never beyond need) "ye are in heaviness through manifold temptations" (made sorrowful by various trials and afflictions); the witnesses note the balance of grace — the believer rejoices in his sure salvation even while grieved by present trials, the joy being deep and lasting, the heaviness brief and measured, sent only as far as needful; so that grief and joy may dwell together in the suffering saint; though now for a season, if need be, ye are in heaviness through manifold temptations. The witnesses note wherein ye greatly rejoice. The witnesses note though now for a season, if need be, ye are in heaviness through manifold temptations.

The witnesses note joy and sorrow joined in the believer — rejoicing in salvation, yet for a season grieved by manifold trials; the joy deep and lasting, the heaviness brief and measured.

So the witnesses make the verse the joy held amid present trials. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer rejoices in his sure salvation even while grieved by trials, the joy lasting and the heaviness brief. The witnesses note the greatly rejoice: "wherein ye greatly rejoice" — Gill notes joy in salvation, hope, inheritance; the greatly rejoice; the in salvation; the hope; the inheritance; the deep joy. The expositors observe the for a season: "though now for a season" — the trials but for a short time; the for a season; the short time; the brief; the the trials; the temporary. The witnesses press the if need be: "if need be" — only so far as God sees necessary; the if need be; the necessary; the only; the never beyond; the measured. The expositors draw the lesson on the heaviness: "ye are in heaviness" — made sorrowful by trials; the heaviness; the sorrowful; the grieved; the the trials; the afflicted. The witnesses observe the manifold temptations: "through manifold temptations" — various trials and afflictions; the manifold temptations; the various; the trials; the afflictions; the many. The expositors note the joy and grief together: grief and joy dwell together in the suffering saint; the joy and grief together; the dwell together; the the saint; the suffering; the balance. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the wherein ye greatly rejoice, the though now for a season if need be ye are in heaviness through manifold temptations, and the joy amid trials. The verse thus records the joy held amid present trials — teaching that the believer rejoices in his sure salvation even while grieved by trials.
7:
"That the trial of your faith, being much more precious than of gold that perisheth, though it be tried with fire, might be found unto praise and honour and glory at the appearing of Jesus Christ:" The witnesses note the precious purpose of the trial of faith. Gill notes this is the principal end God has in afflictive providences, to try the faith of his people, as the faith of Abraham, Job, and Habakkuk was tried. Barnes notes the putting of one's religion to the test, to show what is genuine. The witnesses note Peter declaring that the trial of their faith, more precious than perishing gold tried by fire, might be found unto praise and honour and glory at Christ's appearing; the refining purpose of trial, that proven faith may issue in glory — that Peter reveals the gracious design behind the heaviness (v.6): "that the trial of your faith" (the testing and proving of their faith by affliction) — and he marks its worth: "being much more precious than of gold that perisheth, though it be tried with fire" (faith proved is more precious than gold, for gold, even refined by fire, perishes, but proven faith is imperishable) — and its glorious issue: "might be found unto praise and honour and glory at the appearing of Jesus Christ"; the witnesses note that trial, like the goldsmith's fire, purifies faith and proves it genuine, and such tested faith will be openly rewarded with praise, honour, and glory when Christ appears; so that present trials are the refining of a faith destined for glory; the trial of your faith, being much more precious than of gold that perisheth. The witnesses note the trial of your faith, being much more precious than of gold that perisheth. The witnesses note might be found unto praise and honour and glory at the appearing of Jesus Christ.

The witnesses note the refining purpose of trial — proven faith more precious than perishing gold, tried by fire, to be found unto praise, honour, and glory at Christ's appearing.

So the witnesses make the verse the refining purpose of trial. The lesson the expositors draw is that trial purifies faith and proves it genuine, that it may issue in praise and glory at Christ's appearing. The witnesses note the trial: "that the trial of your faith" — Gill notes God's end in affliction is to try faith; the trial; the of faith; the testing; the proving; the affliction. The expositors observe the more precious than gold: "being much more precious than of gold that perisheth" — proven faith more precious than gold; the more precious than gold; the proven faith; the gold perishes; the imperishable; the worth. The witnesses press the tried with fire: "though it be tried with fire" — purified as gold by the goldsmith's fire; the tried with fire; the the fire; the purified; the the goldsmith; the refined. The expositors draw the lesson on the praise and honour: "might be found unto praise and honour and glory" — issuing in praise, honour, and glory; the praise and honour; the glory; the rewarded; the openly; the the issue. The witnesses observe the at the appearing: "at the appearing of Jesus Christ" — when Christ appears; the at the appearing; the when Christ; the appears; the rewarded; the openly. The expositors note the destined for glory: present trials refine a faith destined for glory; the destined for glory; the present trials; the the faith; the glory. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the might be found unto praise and honour and glory at the appearing of Jesus Christ, and the refining purpose. The verse thus records the refining purpose of trial — teaching that trial purifies faith and proves it genuine, that it may issue in praise and glory at Christ's appearing.
8:
"Whom having not seen, ye love; in whom, though now ye see him not, yet believing, ye rejoice with joy unspeakable and full of glory:" The witnesses note the love and joy of believers toward the unseen Christ. Gill notes Jesus Christ, whom they had never seen with their bodily eyes, being Jews scattered abroad and not in Judea when Christ was on earth, yet, Christ being made known to them through the gospel, they loved him. The witnesses note Peter declaring that, having not seen Christ, they love him, and believing in him though they see him not, they rejoice with joy unspeakable and full of glory; the love and inexpressible joy of faith fixed on the unseen Saviour — that Peter marks the wonder of the believers' faith and affection: "whom having not seen, ye love" (they never beheld Christ with bodily eyes, yet truly love him), "in whom, though now ye see him not, yet believing" (resting on him by faith, not sight), "ye rejoice with joy unspeakable and full of glory"; the witnesses note the heights of this joy — a joy that words cannot utter ("unspeakable") and that is already touched with the glory to come ("full of glory"), springing not from sight but from faith and love toward the unseen Christ; so that the believer's love and joy rest not on seeing but on believing; whom having not seen, ye love. The witnesses note whom having not seen, ye love; in whom, though now ye see him not, yet believing. The witnesses note ye rejoice with joy unspeakable and full of glory.

The witnesses note the love and inexpressible joy of faith — loving the unseen Christ, rejoicing in him though not seen, with joy unspeakable and full of glory; love and joy resting on believing, not sight.

So the witnesses make the verse the joy of faith in the unseen Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer loves and rejoices in Christ by faith, not sight, with a joy unspeakable and full of glory. The witnesses note the not seen, ye love: "whom having not seen, ye love" — Gill notes they never saw Christ, yet love him; the not seen, ye love; the never saw; the yet love; the the unseen; the affection. The expositors observe the through the gospel: Christ made known through the gospel; the through the gospel; the made known; the the preaching; the the unseen; the loved. The witnesses press the believing: "though now ye see him not, yet believing" — resting on him by faith, not sight; the believing; the by faith; the not sight; the resting; the the unseen. The expositors draw the lesson on the joy unspeakable: "ye rejoice with joy unspeakable" — a joy words cannot utter; the joy unspeakable; the words cannot; the inexpressible; the the joy; the unutterable. The witnesses observe the full of glory: "and full of glory" — touched with the glory to come; the full of glory; the the glory; the to come; the already; the glorious. The expositors note the not sight but faith: the joy springs not from sight but from faith and love; the not sight but faith; the from faith; the from love; the the unseen; the believing. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the whom having not seen ye love in whom though now ye see him not yet believing, the ye rejoice with joy unspeakable and full of glory, and the joy of faith. The verse thus records the joy of faith in the unseen Christ — teaching that the believer loves and rejoices in Christ by faith, not sight, with a joy unspeakable and full of glory.
9:
"Receiving the end of your faith, even the salvation of your souls." The witnesses note the great end and reward of faith — salvation. Gill notes "salvation" intends spiritual and eternal salvation, that which God appointed his people to from eternity, obtained by Christ, applied by the Spirit, and to be fully enjoyed hereafter; a just and sufficient ground of joy and rejoicing. The witnesses note Peter declaring that they are receiving the end of their faith, even the salvation of their souls; the salvation of the soul as the goal and present possession of faith — that Peter names what makes that joy (v.8) so well-grounded: "receiving the end of your faith" (faith's great aim, design, and issue) "even the salvation of your souls"; the witnesses note the present tense — believers are even now "receiving" this salvation, possessing it in part by faith and certain to enjoy it in full; for the very purpose toward which faith looks and presses is the eternal salvation of the soul, which God appointed, Christ obtained, and the Spirit applies; so that faith does not press toward an uncertain prize, but is even now laying hold of its sure and glorious end, the saving of the soul; receiving the end of your faith, even the salvation of your souls. The witnesses note receiving the end of your faith. The witnesses note even the salvation of your souls.

The witnesses note the salvation of the soul as faith's great end — even now received in part and certain in full; faith laying hold of its sure and glorious end, the saving of the soul.

So the witnesses make the verse the salvation of the soul as faith's end. The lesson the expositors draw is that faith's great aim and present possession is the eternal salvation of the soul. The witnesses note the end of faith: "receiving the end of your faith" — Gill notes faith's great aim and issue; the end of faith; the the aim; the the design; the the issue; the faith. The expositors observe the receiving: "receiving" — the present tense, possessing it now; the receiving; the present; the now; the in part; the possessing. The witnesses press the salvation of souls: "even the salvation of your souls" — the eternal salvation of the soul; the salvation of souls; the eternal; the the soul; the spiritual; the saved. The expositors draw the lesson on the appointed and obtained: appointed by God, obtained by Christ, applied by the Spirit; the appointed and obtained; the by God; the by Christ; the the Spirit; the applied. The witnesses observe the sure prize: faith presses not toward an uncertain prize but a sure end; the sure prize; the not uncertain; the the end; the certain; the glorious. The expositors note the laying hold: faith is even now laying hold of its end; the laying hold; the even now; the its end; the the soul; the saved. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the receiving the end of your faith, the even the salvation of your souls, and the salvation as faith's end. The verse thus records the salvation of the soul as faith's end — teaching that faith's great aim and present possession is the eternal salvation of the soul.
10:
"Of which salvation the prophets have enquired and searched diligently, who prophesied of the grace that should come unto you:" The witnesses note the prophets' diligent search into the coming salvation. Gill notes the prophets greatly desired the coming of the Saviour and to see him; they longed after the salvation to be accomplished by him, inquired into its nature, and gave an account of it, according to the measure given them. The witnesses note Peter declaring that the prophets enquired and searched diligently into this salvation, prophesying of the grace that should come; the earnest search of the prophets into the salvation now revealed — that Peter, to magnify the greatness of the gospel salvation, points to how the prophets longed and laboured over it: "of which salvation the prophets have enquired and searched diligently" (they made earnest, careful inquiry, longing to understand the salvation they foretold) "who prophesied of the grace that should come unto you"; the witnesses note the honour this puts on the believers' gospel — the very prophets who foretold it did not fully possess it, but searched into it as into a deep and precious mystery, prophesying of the "grace" that was to come to the gospel-age church; so that the salvation now enjoyed was the object of the prophets' longing study; of which salvation the prophets have enquired and searched diligently. The witnesses note of which salvation the prophets have enquired and searched diligently. The witnesses note who prophesied of the grace that should come unto you.

The witnesses note the prophets' diligent search into the coming salvation — longing and labouring over the grace they foretold; the salvation now enjoyed the object of their longing study.

So the witnesses make the verse the prophets' search into the coming salvation. The lesson the expositors draw is that the prophets searched diligently into the salvation they foretold, magnifying the greatness of the gospel. The witnesses note the enquired: "the prophets have enquired and searched diligently" — Gill notes they made earnest, careful inquiry; the enquired; the searched; the diligently; the earnest; the careful. The expositors observe the longed: they longed to understand the salvation they foretold; the longed; the to understand; the foretold; the desired; the the Saviour. The witnesses press the prophesied of grace: "who prophesied of the grace that should come unto you" — they prophesied of the coming grace; the prophesied; the of grace; the should come; the to you; the foretold. The expositors draw the lesson on the not fully possess: the prophets did not fully possess what they foretold; the not fully possess; the the prophets; the foretold; the searched; the longed. The witnesses observe the deep mystery: they searched into it as a deep, precious mystery; the deep mystery; the precious; the searched; the the salvation; the studied. The expositors note the honour on the gospel: this honours the believers' gospel, longed for by prophets; the honour on the gospel; the the believers; the longed for; the prophets; the precious. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the of which salvation the prophets have enquired and searched diligently, the who prophesied of the grace that should come unto you, and the prophets' search. The verse thus records the prophets' search into the coming salvation — teaching that the prophets searched diligently into the salvation they foretold.
11:
"Searching what, or what manner of time the Spirit of Christ which was in them did signify, when it testified beforehand the sufferings of Christ, and the glory that should follow." The witnesses note the prophets' search guided by the Spirit of Christ within them. Gill notes the prophets made diligent inquiry into the exact time Christ should come to work salvation; to whom it was made known that his coming should be before the sceptre departed from Judah. The witnesses note Peter declaring that they searched what time the Spirit of Christ in them signified, testifying beforehand the sufferings of Christ and the glory to follow; the Spirit of Christ in the prophets foretelling his sufferings and glory — that Peter reveals both the object and the author of the prophets' search: they searched "what, or what manner of time" (when, and in what circumstances, the salvation should come) — and the One who moved and informed them was "the Spirit of Christ which was in them" (the Holy Spirit, here called the Spirit of Christ, indwelling and inspiring the prophets) — who "did signify, when it testified beforehand the sufferings of Christ, and the glory that should follow"; the witnesses note the two great themes the Spirit foretold — first "the sufferings of Christ" (his passion and death) and then "the glory that should follow" (his resurrection, exaltation, and kingdom); so that the Old Testament prophets, by Christ's own Spirit, foretold both his cross and his crown; the Spirit of Christ which was in them. The witnesses note the Spirit of Christ which was in them did signify. The witnesses note when it testified beforehand the sufferings of Christ, and the glory that should follow.

The witnesses note the Spirit of Christ in the prophets — foretelling beforehand the sufferings of Christ and the glory to follow; the prophets, by Christ's own Spirit, foretelling his cross and crown.

So the witnesses make the verse the Spirit of Christ foretelling his sufferings and glory. The lesson the expositors draw is that the Spirit of Christ in the prophets foretold beforehand both Christ's sufferings and the glory to follow. The witnesses note the searching the time: "searching what, or what manner of time" — Gill notes they searched the time of Christ's coming; the searching the time; the the time; the when; the the circumstances; the inquired. The expositors observe the Spirit of Christ: "the Spirit of Christ which was in them" — the Holy Spirit, here called the Spirit of Christ, indwelling them; the Spirit of Christ; the the Holy Spirit; the in them; the indwelling; the inspiring. The witnesses press the testified beforehand: "when it testified beforehand" — foretelling in advance; the testified beforehand; the foretelling; the in advance; the the Spirit; the signified. The expositors draw the lesson on the sufferings of Christ: "the sufferings of Christ" — his passion and death; the sufferings of Christ; the his passion; the his death; the the cross; the foretold. The witnesses observe the glory to follow: "and the glory that should follow" — his resurrection, exaltation, kingdom; the glory to follow; the resurrection; the exaltation; the the kingdom; the the crown. The expositors note the cross and crown: the prophets foretold both his cross and his crown; the cross and crown; the both; the the cross; the the crown; the foretold. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the Spirit of Christ which was in them did signify, the when it testified beforehand the sufferings of Christ and the glory that should follow, and the Spirit foretelling. The verse thus records the Spirit of Christ foretelling his sufferings and glory — teaching that the Spirit of Christ in the prophets foretold both Christ's sufferings and the glory to follow.
12:
"Unto whom it was revealed, that not unto themselves, but unto us they did minister the things, which are now reported unto you by them that have preached the gospel unto you with the Holy Ghost sent down from heaven; which things the angels desire to look into." The witnesses note that the prophets ministered for us, and the angels' longing to look into these things. Gill notes it was revealed to them what time Christ should come, what sort of times they would be, and what cruel sufferings the Messiah should undergo. The witnesses note Peter declaring that it was revealed to the prophets that they ministered not to themselves but to us, things now preached by the gospel with the Holy Ghost, which the angels desire to look into; the prophets serving the gospel church, and the angels' wondering longing — that Peter shows the prophets were shown they served a future age: "not unto themselves, but unto us they did minister the things" (their prophecies were for the benefit of the gospel-age church) "which are now reported unto you by them that have preached the gospel unto you with the Holy Ghost sent down from heaven" (the same things now openly preached, in the Spirit's power) — and he adds a striking note of the glory of this gospel: "which things the angels desire to look into"; the witnesses note the wonder — even the holy angels stoop and gaze with eager longing into the mysteries of redemption, as into something surpassing and ever new; so that the gospel the believers possess is a thing prophets served and angels marvel at; which things the angels desire to look into. The witnesses note not unto themselves, but unto us they did minister the things. The witnesses note which things the angels desire to look into.

The witnesses note the prophets serving the gospel church, not themselves — the things now preached in the Spirit's power, which even the holy angels desire to look into with eager longing.

So the witnesses make the verse the prophets serving the gospel and the angels' longing. The lesson the expositors draw is that the prophets ministered for the gospel age, and even the angels stoop to gaze into the mysteries of redemption. The witnesses note the not themselves but us: "not unto themselves, but unto us they did minister" — Gill notes their prophecies were for the gospel church; the not themselves but us; the for us; the the gospel church; the ministered; the future age. The expositors observe the now reported: "which are now reported unto you" — the same things now openly preached; the now reported; the openly preached; the the gospel; the the same; the proclaimed. The witnesses press the with the Holy Ghost: "with the Holy Ghost sent down from heaven" — in the Spirit's power; the with the Holy Ghost; the the Spirit; the power; the sent down; the from heaven. The expositors draw the lesson on the angels desire: "which things the angels desire to look into" — even angels long to look in; the angels desire; the the angels; the long; the look into; the gaze. The witnesses observe the eager longing: they gaze with eager, wondering longing; the eager longing; the wondering; the gaze; the stoop; the marvel. The expositors note the surpassing: the mysteries of redemption surpass and are ever new; the surpassing; the redemption; the ever new; the wonder. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the which things the angels desire to look into, and the gospel prophets served. The verse thus records the prophets serving the gospel and the angels' longing — teaching that the prophets ministered for the gospel age, and even the angels gaze into the mysteries of redemption.
13:
"Wherefore gird up the loins of your mind, be sober, and hope to the end for the grace that is to be brought unto you at the revelation of Jesus Christ;" The witnesses note the call to mental readiness, sobriety, and steadfast hope. Gill notes "gird up the loins of your mind" with the girdle of truth; since angels desire to look into these mysteries of grace, the saints should apply their minds and diligently attend to them, against all loose and vagrant thoughts. The witnesses note Peter charging that they gird up the loins of their mind, be sober, and hope to the end for the grace to be brought at Christ's revelation; the call to a girded, sober mind and a hope fixed firmly on the coming grace — that Peter, drawing the practical conclusion ("wherefore") from the glory of their salvation, calls for three things: "gird up the loins of your mind" (like a man girding his loose robes to run or work, so brace and collect the mind for action, free of loose, distracting thoughts), "be sober" (self-controlled, watchful, temperate), and "hope to the end" (set a firm, persevering hope) "for the grace that is to be brought unto you at the revelation of Jesus Christ"; the witnesses note that the believer is to fix his hope wholly and steadfastly on the grace and glory to be brought at Christ's appearing, with a mind girded and sober for the journey there; gird up the loins of your mind, be sober, and hope to the end. The witnesses note gird up the loins of your mind, be sober. The witnesses note hope to the end for the grace that is to be brought unto you at the revelation of Jesus Christ.

The witnesses note the call to a girded, sober mind and a steadfast hope — fixed wholly on the grace to be brought at Christ's revelation; the mind braced for action, hope set to the end.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to a girded mind and steadfast hope. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer is to brace and collect his mind, be sober, and fix a firm hope on the grace coming at Christ's revelation. The witnesses note the gird up: "gird up the loins of your mind" — Gill notes brace the mind, against loose thoughts; the gird up; the brace; the the mind; the loose thoughts; the collect. The expositors observe the like a man girding: like a man girding his robes for action; the like a man girding; the his robes; the for action; the to run; the brace. The witnesses press the be sober: "be sober" — self-controlled, watchful, temperate; the be sober; the self-controlled; the watchful; the temperate; the alert. The expositors draw the lesson on the hope to the end: "and hope to the end" — a firm, persevering hope; the hope to the end; the firm; the persevering; the steadfast; the to the end. The witnesses observe the the grace coming: "for the grace that is to be brought unto you" — the grace coming at Christ's appearing; the the grace coming; the at his appearing; the brought; the the revelation; the coming. The expositors note the fixed wholly: hope fixed wholly and steadfastly on that grace; the fixed wholly; the steadfastly; the hope; the set. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the hope to the end for the grace that is to be brought unto you at the revelation of Jesus Christ, and the girded mind and hope. The verse thus records the call to a girded mind and steadfast hope — teaching that the believer is to brace his mind, be sober, and fix a firm hope on the grace coming at Christ's revelation.
14:
"As obedient children, not fashioning yourselves according to the former lusts in your ignorance:" The witnesses note the call to obedient children not to be conformed to former lusts. Gill notes "obedient children," or "children of obedience"; seeing they were children of God by adopting grace, and brought to the obedience of faith, they ought to live accordingly. The witnesses note Peter charging that, as obedient children, they not fashion themselves according to their former lusts in their ignorance; the call of God's children to be unlike their old sinful selves — that Peter, pressing holiness, addresses the believers by their new character: "as obedient children" (children of God marked by obedience, born to a new life of submission to him) — and bids them not to be "fashioning yourselves according to the former lusts" (not shaping their lives by the sinful desires that ruled them before) "in your ignorance"; the witnesses note the contrast between the old life and the new — those lusts belonged to the time of their "ignorance," before they knew God, and are not to be the pattern of the obedient child's life now; so that conversion makes a clean break with the former sinful mould; not fashioning yourselves according to the former lusts in your ignorance. The witnesses note as obedient children. The witnesses note not fashioning yourselves according to the former lusts in your ignorance.

The witnesses note the call of God's obedient children not to be conformed to their former lusts — desires that belonged to their ignorance; conversion making a clean break with the old sinful mould.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to be unlike the old self. The lesson the expositors draw is that God's obedient children are not to shape their lives by the lusts of their former ignorance. The witnesses note the obedient children: "as obedient children" — Gill notes children of God marked by obedience; the obedient children; the of God; the obedience; the the new character; the born. The expositors observe the new life: born to a new life of submission to God; the new life; the submission; the to God; the the new; the born. The witnesses press the not fashioning: "not fashioning yourselves" — not shaping their lives by sinful desire; the not fashioning; the not shaping; the sinful desire; the the pattern; the conformed. The expositors draw the lesson on the former lusts: "according to the former lusts" — the sinful desires that ruled before; the former lusts; the sinful desires; the ruled before; the the old; the pattern. The witnesses observe the in ignorance: "in your ignorance" — belonging to the time before they knew God; the in ignorance; the before they knew; the God; the the old time; the unconverted. The expositors note the clean break: conversion makes a clean break with the old mould; the clean break; the conversion; the the old mould; the unlike; the new. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the as obedient children, the not fashioning yourselves according to the former lusts in your ignorance, and the unlike the old self. The verse thus records the call to be unlike the old self — teaching that God's obedient children are not to shape their lives by the lusts of their former ignorance.
15:
"But as he which hath called you is holy, so be ye holy in all manner of conversation;" The witnesses note the call to holiness modelled on the holy God who called them. Gill notes "he which hath called you is holy" is a description of God the Father, who called them not merely externally by the word, but internally and efficaciously by his Spirit and grace, and called them to holiness. The witnesses note Peter charging that, as he who called them is holy, so they be holy in all manner of conduct; the holiness of God set as the pattern of the believer's whole life — that Peter gives the positive side of the charge and its great motive: "but as he which hath called you is holy" (the God who effectually called them is himself holy, perfectly pure) "so be ye holy in all manner of conversation" (be holy too, and that not in part but in your whole manner of life, in every action and relation); the witnesses note the loftiness of the standard — the believer's holiness is to be patterned on God's own holiness, the holy God calling his people to be like himself; and it is to reach "all manner of conversation," every part of life, not some narrow corner only; so that the called are to bear the family likeness of their holy God in all they do; so be ye holy in all manner of conversation. The witnesses note as he which hath called you is holy. The witnesses note so be ye holy in all manner of conversation.

The witnesses note the call to holiness patterned on the holy God who called them — to be holy in the whole manner of life; the called bearing the family likeness of their holy God.

So the witnesses make the verse the holiness of God as the believer's pattern. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer is to be holy in all his life, patterned on the holiness of the God who called him. The witnesses note the he who called: "as he which hath called you is holy" — Gill notes God the Father, who effectually called them; the he who called; the God the Father; the effectually; the called; the holy. The expositors observe the himself holy: the God who called is himself holy, perfectly pure; the himself holy; the perfectly pure; the the standard; the the pattern; the holy. The witnesses press the be ye holy: "so be ye holy" — be holy too, like him; the be ye holy; the like him; the holy; the the call; the patterned. The expositors draw the lesson on the all manner: "in all manner of conversation" — in the whole manner of life; the all manner; the the whole life; the every action; the every relation; the not part. The witnesses observe the family likeness: the called bear the family likeness of their holy God; the family likeness; the their holy God; the like himself; the the called; the bear. The expositors note the every part: holiness is to reach every part of life, not a corner; the every part; the every part of life; the not a corner; the all; the whole. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the as he which hath called you is holy, the so be ye holy in all manner of conversation, and the holiness patterned on God. The verse thus records the holiness of God as the believer's pattern — teaching that the believer is to be holy in all his life, patterned on the holiness of the God who called him.
16:
"Because it is written, Be ye holy; for I am holy." The witnesses note the scriptural ground of the call to holiness. Barnes notes this command was addressed at first to the Israelites, but is with equal propriety addressed to Christians as the professed people of God; the foundation of the command is that they professed to be his. The witnesses note Peter grounding the call to holiness in the written word, "Be ye holy; for I am holy"; the divine command and reason for holiness drawn from Scripture — that Peter clinches his exhortation by an appeal to the law: "because it is written, Be ye holy; for I am holy" (citing God's own word in Leviticus); the witnesses note the force of this — the call to holiness is no mere apostolic counsel but God's standing command, and the reason given is the highest possible: God's own holiness ("for I am holy"); so that the believer's holiness is required by the express word of God and grounded in the very nature of the God whose people they are — they must be holy because he who owns them is holy; Be ye holy; for I am holy. The witnesses note because it is written, Be ye holy. The witnesses note for I am holy.

The witnesses note the scriptural ground of holiness — "Be ye holy; for I am holy"; God's standing command, grounded in the highest reason, his own holiness, binding on his people.

So the witnesses make the verse the scriptural command and reason for holiness. The lesson the expositors draw is that holiness is God's express command, grounded in his own holiness, binding on his people. The witnesses note the it is written: "because it is written" — Peter cites God's own word in Leviticus; the it is written; the cites; the the word; the Leviticus; the the law. The expositors observe the standing command: the call is no mere counsel but God's standing command; the standing command; the not counsel; the God's command; the express; the binding. The witnesses press the be ye holy: "Be ye holy" — the divine command to holiness; the be ye holy; the the command; the holiness; the divine; the required. The expositors draw the lesson on the for I am holy: "for I am holy" — the reason is God's own holiness; the for I am holy; the the reason; the God's holiness; the the highest; the his nature. The witnesses observe Barnes's note: Barnes notes the command, first to Israel, fits the Christian people of God; the Barnes; the to Israel; the the Christian; the the people of God; the fits. The expositors note the he who owns them: they must be holy because he who owns them is holy; the he who owns them; the holy; the his people; the required; the grounded. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the because it is written Be ye holy, the for I am holy, and the command and reason. The verse thus records the scriptural command and reason for holiness — teaching that holiness is God's express command, grounded in his own holiness, binding on his people.
17:
"And if ye call on the Father, who without respect of persons judgeth according to every man's work, pass the time of your sojourning here in fear:" The witnesses note the call to live in reverent fear before the impartial Father and Judge. Gill notes "if ye call on the Father," or "seeing" ye do; invocation of God includes the whole worship of him, the performance of every outward duty and exercise of every inward grace. The witnesses note Peter charging that, since they call on the Father who judges impartially according to each man's work, they pass their sojourning here in fear; the reverent fear that befits those who own an impartial Father and Judge — that Peter draws another motive to holiness from the character of the God they call Father: "if ye call on the Father" (since you invoke and worship God as your Father) "who without respect of persons judgeth according to every man's work" (who is no respecter of persons, but judges each impartially by his works) — therefore "pass the time of your sojourning here in fear"; the witnesses note the kind of fear meant — not slavish dread, but a reverent, holy, filial fear, a careful walking that dreads to offend so holy and impartial a Father; and "the time of your sojourning here" marks the believer's brief pilgrimage on earth, to be spent in this reverent fear; pass the time of your sojourning here in fear. The witnesses note if ye call on the Father, who without respect of persons judgeth according to every man's work. The witnesses note pass the time of your sojourning here in fear.

The witnesses note the reverent fear befitting those who call on an impartial Father and Judge — passing their brief pilgrimage in a holy, filial fear that dreads to offend so holy a Father.

So the witnesses make the verse the reverent fear before the impartial Father. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer, owning an impartial Father and Judge, is to pass his pilgrimage in reverent, filial fear. The witnesses note the call on the Father: "if ye call on the Father" — Gill notes invoking and worshipping God as Father; the call on the Father; the invoking; the worshipping; the as Father; the seeing ye do. The expositors observe the without respect: "who without respect of persons judgeth" — no respecter of persons, impartial; the without respect; the impartial; the no respecter; the judges; the each. The witnesses press the every man's work: "according to every man's work" — judges each by his works; the every man's work; the by his works; the each; the impartial; the judged. The expositors draw the lesson on the in fear: "pass the time of your sojourning here in fear" — pass the pilgrimage in fear; the in fear; the the pilgrimage; the sojourning; the pass; the reverent. The witnesses observe the filial fear: not slavish dread but reverent, filial fear; the filial fear; the not slavish; the reverent; the holy; the careful. The expositors note the dreads to offend: a fear that dreads to offend so holy a Father; the dreads to offend; the so holy; the the Father; the careful; the walk. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the if ye call on the Father who without respect of persons judgeth according to every man's work, the pass the time of your sojourning here in fear, and the reverent fear. The verse thus records the reverent fear before the impartial Father — teaching that the believer, owning an impartial Father and Judge, is to pass his pilgrimage in reverent, filial fear.
18:
"Forasmuch as ye know that ye were not redeemed with corruptible things, as silver and gold, from your vain conversation received by tradition from your fathers;" The witnesses note the argument for holiness drawn from the costly redemption. Gill notes they knew this from the Scriptures, the testimony of the Spirit, his work on the soul, and the application of redemption's benefits; Barnes notes this is an argument for a holy life, derived from the manner of their redemption. The witnesses note Peter reasoning that they know they were not redeemed with corruptible things, as silver and gold, from their vain manner of life inherited from their fathers; the costliness of redemption as a motive for holiness — that Peter presses holiness by the priceless means of their redemption: "forasmuch as ye know" (it is a known and certain truth) "that ye were not redeemed with corruptible things, as silver and gold" (no perishable price, however precious, bought them) "from your vain conversation received by tradition from your fathers" (delivered from the empty, worthless manner of life they had inherited, the traditional but vain way of their ancestors); the witnesses note the force — to be bought out of so worthless a former life at no common price binds the redeemed to holiness; silver and gold, the most precious of perishing things, could not redeem them, which sets up the contrast of the next verse; ye were not redeemed with corruptible things, as silver and gold. The witnesses note ye were not redeemed with corruptible things, as silver and gold. The witnesses note from your vain conversation received by tradition from your fathers.

The witnesses note the argument from costly redemption — not redeemed with silver and gold from their vain inherited manner of life; the priceless means of redemption binding the redeemed to holiness.

So the witnesses make the verse the costliness of redemption as a motive. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believers were redeemed from a worthless former life not by perishable silver or gold, binding them to holiness. The witnesses note the ye know: "forasmuch as ye know" — Gill notes a known and certain truth; the ye know; the certain; the known; the from Scripture; the the Spirit. The expositors observe the not corruptible: "ye were not redeemed with corruptible things" — no perishable price; the not corruptible; the perishable; the no price; the bought; the redeemed. The witnesses press the silver and gold: "as silver and gold" — the most precious of perishing things; the silver and gold; the most precious; the perishing; the could not; the the price. The expositors draw the lesson on the vain conversation: "from your vain conversation" — the empty, worthless manner of life; the vain conversation; the empty; the worthless; the the manner of life; the delivered. The witnesses observe the from the fathers: "received by tradition from your fathers" — inherited from their ancestors; the from the fathers; the inherited; the tradition; the the ancestors; the the old. The expositors note the binds to holiness: redemption at no common price binds the redeemed to holiness; the binds to holiness; the no common price; the the redeemed; the holiness; the bound. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the ye were not redeemed with corruptible things as silver and gold, the from your vain conversation received by tradition from your fathers, and the costly redemption. The verse thus records the costliness of redemption as a motive — teaching that the believers were redeemed not by perishable silver or gold, binding them to holiness.
19:
"But with the precious blood of Christ, as of a lamb without blemish and without spot:" The witnesses note the precious blood of Christ as the price of redemption. Gill notes Christ was prophesied of as a Redeemer; the apostle here attributes redemption to the blood of Christ, whose blood is the same with ours, only not tainted with sin, the blood of an innocent and spotless Lamb. The witnesses note Peter declaring that they were redeemed with the precious blood of Christ, as of a lamb without blemish and without spot; the priceless blood of the spotless Lamb as the ransom-price — that Peter names the true and infinitely precious price of redemption, in glorious contrast to silver and gold: "but with the precious blood of Christ" (the blood of the Son of God, of inestimable worth and dignity) "as of a lamb without blemish and without spot"; the witnesses note the rich typology — Christ is the true Paschal and sacrificial Lamb, answering to the spotless lambs of the law that had to be "without blemish," for he was perfectly sinless, holy, and pure; and it was this precious, spotless blood, not corruptible metal, that purchased the believers' redemption; so that the costliness of the ransom both magnifies redemption and binds the redeemed to holy living; the precious blood of Christ, as of a lamb without blemish and without spot. The witnesses note but with the precious blood of Christ. The witnesses note as of a lamb without blemish and without spot.

The witnesses note the precious blood of the spotless Lamb as the ransom — Christ the true Paschal Lamb "without blemish," whose priceless blood, not corruptible metal, purchased redemption.

So the witnesses make the verse the precious blood of Christ as the ransom-price. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believers were redeemed by the priceless blood of the spotless Lamb, Christ, not by perishable metal. The witnesses note the precious blood: "but with the precious blood of Christ" — Gill notes the blood of the Son of God, of inestimable worth; the precious blood; the of Christ; the the Son of God; the inestimable; the the price. The expositors observe the contrast: in glorious contrast to silver and gold; the contrast; the silver and gold; the precious; the the price; the redemption. The witnesses press the the Lamb: "as of a lamb" — Christ the true Paschal and sacrificial Lamb; the the Lamb; the the Paschal Lamb; the sacrificial; the the type; the Christ. The expositors draw the lesson on the without blemish: "without blemish and without spot" — answering the spotless lambs of the law; the without blemish; the without spot; the spotless; the the law; the sinless. The witnesses observe the perfectly sinless: he was perfectly sinless, holy, and pure; the perfectly sinless; the holy; the pure; the spotless; the the Lamb. The expositors note the binds to holiness: the costly ransom magnifies redemption and binds to holiness; the binds to holiness; the costly ransom; the magnifies; the redemption; the bound. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the but with the precious blood of Christ, the as of a lamb without blemish and without spot, and the precious blood. The verse thus records the precious blood of Christ as the ransom-price — teaching that the believers were redeemed by the priceless blood of the spotless Lamb, not by perishable metal.
20:
"Who verily was foreordained before the foundation of the world, but was manifest in these last times for you," The witnesses note that Christ the Redeemer was foreordained before the world's foundation. Gill notes "foreordained," or "foreknown," that is, by God; which intends not barely his prescience, but such a previous knowledge joined with love and affection to him, not merely as his Son but as Mediator. The witnesses note Peter declaring that Christ was foreordained before the foundation of the world, but manifest in these last times for them; the eternal foreordination of Christ the Redeemer, revealed in the last times — that Peter sets the redeeming Lamb in the vast span of God's eternal purpose: Christ "verily was foreordained before the foundation of the world" (designated and set apart as the Redeemer in God's eternal counsel, before time began, no afterthought but the eternal plan) "but was manifest in these last times for you"; the witnesses note the two ages joined — Christ was eternally ordained to be the sacrifice, yet only "manifest" (made openly known, having come in the flesh and accomplished redemption) "in these last times," the gospel age — and that "for you," for the very believers Peter addresses; so that the Lamb of God was both eternally appointed and now historically revealed, all for his people's sake; who verily was foreordained before the foundation of the world. The witnesses note who verily was foreordained before the foundation of the world. The witnesses note but was manifest in these last times for you.

The witnesses note Christ the Redeemer foreordained before the world's foundation — no afterthought but the eternal plan — yet manifest in these last times, all for his people's sake.

So the witnesses make the verse the eternal foreordination of Christ revealed in the last times. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ was designated the Redeemer in God's eternal counsel, but openly manifest in the gospel age for his people. The witnesses note the foreordained: "who verily was foreordained before the foundation of the world" — Gill notes designated the Redeemer in God's eternal counsel; the foreordained; the before the foundation; the eternal counsel; the the Redeemer; the appointed. The expositors observe the no afterthought: no afterthought but the eternal plan; the no afterthought; the the eternal plan; the before time; the appointed; the purposed. The witnesses press the foreknown with love: Gill notes foreknown with love and affection, as Mediator; the foreknown with love; the love; the affection; the as Mediator; the the Son. The expositors draw the lesson on the manifest: "but was manifest in these last times" — made openly known in the gospel age; the manifest; the made known; the the gospel age; the come in the flesh; the revealed. The witnesses observe the for you: "for you" — for the very believers addressed; the for you; the the believers; the addressed; the his people; the for their sake. The expositors note the both ordained and revealed: the Lamb both eternally appointed and now revealed; the both ordained and revealed; the eternally appointed; the now revealed; the the Lamb; the for his people. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the who verily was foreordained before the foundation of the world, the but was manifest in these last times for you, and the eternal foreordination. The verse thus records the eternal foreordination of Christ revealed in the last times — teaching that Christ was designated the Redeemer in God's eternal counsel, but manifest in the gospel age for his people.
21:
"Who by him do believe in God, that raised him up from the dead, and gave him glory; that your faith and hope might be in God." The witnesses note that through Christ believers' faith and hope are placed in God. Gill notes Christ, as God, is the object of faith; as Mediator, he is the way to the Father, by whom men come to God, believe in him, and lay hold on him as their covenant God and Father. The witnesses note Peter declaring that by Christ they believe in God, who raised him from the dead and gave him glory, that their faith and hope might be in God; faith and hope reaching God through the risen and glorified Christ — that Peter shows how Christ brings his people to God: it is "by him" (through Christ, the only way to the Father) that they "do believe in God" — and the God they believe in is the One "that raised him up from the dead, and gave him glory" (who raised and exalted Christ, the resurrection being God's seal on his finished work); and the gracious purpose: "that your faith and hope might be in God"; the witnesses note that the resurrection and exaltation of Christ are the very ground on which the believer's faith and hope rest securely in God, for the God who raised and glorified the Redeemer is the sure object of their trust; that your faith and hope might be in God. The witnesses note who by him do believe in God, that raised him up from the dead, and gave him glory. The witnesses note that your faith and hope might be in God.

The witnesses note faith and hope reaching God through the risen Christ — believing in the God who raised and glorified him; the resurrection the ground on which faith and hope rest in God.

So the witnesses make the verse faith and hope in God through the risen Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is that through Christ believers' faith and hope rest in the God who raised and glorified him. The witnesses note the by him believe: "who by him do believe in God" — Gill notes through Christ, the way to the Father; the by him believe; the through Christ; the the way; the the Father; the believe. The expositors observe the raised him: "that raised him up from the dead" — God raised Christ; the raised him; the from the dead; the God; the resurrection; the seal. The witnesses press the gave him glory: "and gave him glory" — exalted Christ; the gave him glory; the exalted; the glorified; the God; the the work. The expositors draw the lesson on the faith and hope in God: "that your faith and hope might be in God" — that faith and hope rest in God; the faith and hope in God; the rest; the in God; the the purpose; the secure. The witnesses observe the the ground: the resurrection is the ground of that faith; the the ground; the the resurrection; the of faith; the rests; the secure. The expositors note the sure object: the God who raised the Redeemer is the sure object of trust; the sure object; the the God; the raised; the the Redeemer; the trust. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the who by him do believe in God that raised him up from the dead and gave him glory, the that your faith and hope might be in God, and faith and hope in God. The verse thus records faith and hope in God through the risen Christ — teaching that through Christ believers' faith and hope rest in the God who raised and glorified him.
22:
"Seeing ye have purified your souls in obeying the truth through the Spirit unto unfeigned love of the brethren, see that ye love one another with a pure heart fervently:" The witnesses note the call to fervent brotherly love grounded in the soul's purification. Gill notes the apostle passes to brotherly love, the ground of which he makes to be the purification of their souls; which supposes they had been impure, and indeed their whole persons were so by nature. The witnesses note Peter charging that, having purified their souls in obeying the truth through the Spirit unto unfeigned love, they love one another with a pure heart fervently; the fervent, sincere brotherly love that flows from a purified soul — that Peter grounds the duty of love in the work already wrought in them: "seeing ye have purified your souls in obeying the truth through the Spirit" (their souls cleansed, by the Spirit's power, through their obedient reception of the gospel) "unto unfeigned love of the brethren" (the very aim and fruit of that purifying being a genuine, unhypocritical love of fellow believers) — therefore, "see that ye love one another with a pure heart fervently"; the witnesses note the two marks of this love — it is to be from "a pure heart" (sincere, cleansed of selfishness and guile) and "fervently" (warm, earnest, intense, not cold or slack); so that the purified soul is to overflow in warm, sincere love to the brethren; see that ye love one another with a pure heart fervently. The witnesses note seeing ye have purified your souls in obeying the truth through the Spirit unto unfeigned love of the brethren. The witnesses note see that ye love one another with a pure heart fervently.

The witnesses note the call to fervent, sincere brotherly love flowing from a purified soul — cleansed by the Spirit through obeying the truth; love from a pure heart, warm and earnest.

So the witnesses make the verse the fervent love flowing from a purified soul. The lesson the expositors draw is that the soul purified by obeying the truth is to overflow in warm, sincere love to the brethren. The witnesses note the purified souls: "ye have purified your souls in obeying the truth through the Spirit" — Gill notes the soul cleansed by the Spirit through the gospel; the purified souls; the cleansed; the by the Spirit; the the truth; the obeying. The expositors observe the unto love: "unto unfeigned love of the brethren" — the aim being a genuine love of believers; the unto love; the unfeigned; the the brethren; the genuine; the the fruit. The witnesses press the love one another: "see that ye love one another" — therefore love one another; the love one another; the the duty; the the brethren; the love; the the charge. The expositors draw the lesson on the pure heart: "with a pure heart" — sincere, cleansed of guile; the pure heart; the sincere; the cleansed; the no guile; the no selfishness. The witnesses observe the fervently: "fervently" — warm, earnest, intense; the fervently; the warm; the earnest; the intense; the not slack. The expositors note the overflow: the purified soul overflows in warm, sincere love; the overflow; the the purified soul; the warm; the sincere; the love. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the seeing ye have purified your souls in obeying the truth through the Spirit unto unfeigned love of the brethren, the see that ye love one another with a pure heart fervently, and the fervent love. The verse thus records the fervent love flowing from a purified soul — teaching that the soul purified by obeying the truth is to overflow in warm, sincere love to the brethren.
23:
"Being born again, not of corruptible seed, but of incorruptible, by the word of God, which liveth and abideth for ever." The witnesses note the new birth by the incorruptible seed of God's word. Gill notes they were born again of God, according to his abundant mercy, by the resurrection of Christ, to a lively hope; and being brethren in a spiritual relation, they ought to love as brethren, children of the same Father. The witnesses note Peter declaring that they are born again, not of corruptible but of incorruptible seed, by the word of God which lives and abides for ever; the new birth by the abiding word a further ground of brotherly love — that Peter gives a further and deeper ground for brotherly love (v.22): their common new birth — "being born again, not of corruptible seed, but of incorruptible" (regenerated not by the perishable seed of natural generation, but by an imperishable spiritual seed) "by the word of God, which liveth and abideth for ever"; the witnesses note that the means and seed of this new birth is "the word of God," the living and abiding gospel, which the Spirit uses to beget souls anew — and because that word is itself living and eternal, the life it produces is likewise imperishable; so that those born again of the same incorruptible seed and abiding word are truly brethren, bound to love one another; being born again... by the word of God, which liveth and abideth for ever. The witnesses note being born again, not of corruptible seed, but of incorruptible. The witnesses note by the word of God, which liveth and abideth for ever.

The witnesses note the new birth by the incorruptible seed of God's living and abiding word — a deeper ground of brotherly love; the life it produces imperishable, like the word itself.

So the witnesses make the verse the new birth by the abiding word. The lesson the expositors draw is that believers are born again by the incorruptible seed of God's living word, a ground of brotherly love. The witnesses note the born again: "being born again" — Gill notes regenerated of God; the born again; the regenerated; the of God; the the new birth; the begotten. The expositors observe the not corruptible seed: "not of corruptible seed, but of incorruptible" — not perishable but imperishable seed; the not corruptible seed; the perishable; the incorruptible; the imperishable; the spiritual. The witnesses press the the word of God: "by the word of God" — the means and seed is God's word; the the word of God; the the means; the the seed; the the gospel; the the Spirit uses. The expositors draw the lesson on the liveth and abideth: "which liveth and abideth for ever" — the living, eternal word; the liveth and abideth; the living; the eternal; the abiding; the for ever. The witnesses observe the imperishable life: the life it produces is imperishable, like the word; the imperishable life; the the life; the imperishable; the like the word; the eternal. The expositors note the truly brethren: those born of the same seed are truly brethren; the truly brethren; the the same seed; the the word; the bound; the love. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the being born again not of corruptible seed but of incorruptible, the by the word of God which liveth and abideth for ever, and the new birth by the word. The verse thus records the new birth by the abiding word — teaching that believers are born again by the incorruptible seed of God's living word.
24:
"For all flesh is as grass, and all the glory of man as the flower of grass. The grass withereth, and the flower thereof falleth away:" The witnesses note the frailty and fading of all human flesh and glory. Gill notes all men, as born of corruptible seed, are frail, mortal, and perishing; they spring up like grass, look beautiful for a while, but are soon cut down by death and wither away.
