The witnesses note Peter citing that all flesh is as grass and all the glory of man as the flower of grass, which withers and falls away; the fading of all earthly things set against the abiding word — that Peter, having spoken of the imperishable word (v.23), sets against it the perishableness of all human things, citing Isaiah: "all flesh is as grass" (man in his whole natural being is frail, short-lived, like grass that springs up and is gone) "and all the glory of man as the flower of grass" (even man's finest glory, beauty, and achievement is like the brief flower of the field) — "the grass withereth, and the flower thereof falleth away"; the witnesses note the lesson — all that is of nature, the man and his glory alike, quickly fades and dies, so that there is nothing abiding to rest upon in the flesh; the contrast prepares the great affirmation of the next verse, that the word alone endures; the grass withereth, and the flower thereof falleth away. The witnesses note all flesh is as grass, and all the glory of man as the flower of grass. The witnesses note the grass withereth, and the flower thereof falleth away.

The witnesses note the fading of all flesh and human glory — like grass and its flower that wither and fall; nothing abiding in the flesh, set against the word that alone endures.

So the witnesses make the verse the fading of all earthly things. The lesson the expositors draw is that all human flesh and glory quickly fades and dies, so nothing in the flesh abides. The witnesses note the all flesh: "all flesh is as grass" — Gill notes man is frail, mortal, like grass; the all flesh; the frail; the mortal; the like grass; the perishing. The expositors observe the springs up: man springs up like grass and is soon gone; the springs up; the like grass; the soon gone; the cut down; the death. The witnesses press the glory of man: "and all the glory of man as the flower of grass" — man's finest glory is like the brief flower; the glory of man; the the flower; the brief; the beauty; the fades. The expositors draw the lesson on the withereth: "the grass withereth" — the grass withers; the withereth; the the grass; the withers; the dies; the fades. The witnesses observe the falleth away: "and the flower thereof falleth away" — the flower falls; the falleth away; the the flower; the falls; the gone; the fades. The expositors note the nothing abiding: nothing in the flesh abides to rest upon; the nothing abiding; the in the flesh; the fades; the dies; the no rest. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the all flesh is as grass and all the glory of man as the flower of grass, the the grass withereth and the flower thereof falleth away, and the fading of all flesh. The verse thus records the fading of all earthly things — teaching that all human flesh and glory quickly fades and dies, so nothing in the flesh abides.
25:
"But the word of the Lord endureth for ever. And this is the word which by the gospel is preached unto you." The witnesses note the everlasting endurance of the word of the Lord. Gill notes though men die, and ministers of the word too, and everything in the world is uncertain and passing away, yet the word of the Lord, the gospel of Christ, is settled for ever and will continue. The witnesses note Peter declaring that the word of the Lord endures for ever, and that this is the word preached to them by the gospel; the imperishable, abiding word now preached as the seed of the new birth — that Peter completes the contrast: against all the fading of flesh and glory (v.24) stands the changeless word — "but the word of the Lord endureth for ever" (the gospel, God's word, abides unchanging while all else passes) — and he applies it: "and this is the word which by the gospel is preached unto you"; the witnesses note the comfort and force — the very word now preached to them in the gospel is that eternal, abiding word of God by which they were born again (v.23); so that, while all the world withers, the believer is begotten and built upon a word that never passes away, the sure and lasting foundation of his hope; the word of the Lord endureth for ever. The witnesses note but the word of the Lord endureth for ever. The witnesses note this is the word which by the gospel is preached unto you.

The witnesses note the everlasting word of the Lord — abiding unchanging while all flesh fades; the very word preached in the gospel, by which they were born again, a sure and lasting foundation.

So the witnesses make the verse the imperishable, abiding word. The lesson the expositors draw is that the word of the Lord endures for ever, the very gospel preached to them, the foundation of their hope. The witnesses note the endureth: "but the word of the Lord endureth for ever" — Gill notes the gospel abides while all passes; the endureth; the for ever; the the gospel; the abides; the unchanging. The expositors observe the all else passes: while men and all the world pass away; the all else passes; the men die; the the world; the passing; the fades. The witnesses press the this is the word: "and this is the word which by the gospel is preached unto you" — the very word preached to them; the this is the word; the the gospel; the preached; the to them; the the same. The expositors draw the lesson on the born again by it: it is the word by which they were born again; the born again by it; the the new birth; the the seed; the the word; the begotten. The witnesses observe the never passes: the believer is built on a word that never passes; the never passes; the the word; the abides; the the foundation; the lasting. The expositors note the foundation of hope: the sure and lasting foundation of his hope; the foundation of hope; the sure; the lasting; the the word; the his hope. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the but the word of the Lord endureth for ever, the this is the word which by the gospel is preached unto you, and the abiding word. The verse thus records the imperishable, abiding word — teaching that the word of the Lord endures for ever, the very gospel preached to them, the foundation of their hope.
